facts_disclosed: Started eating fish again — pescatarian now

turns.content: ugh I've been getting more migraines again. like I thought I had them under control but this past month has been bad. I'm trying to figure out if it's food-related or just stress

Ugh, that's miserable — migraines are one of those things that genuinely tank your quality of life in ways people who don't get them don't always appreciate. It's hard to function when you're either having one or anxious about having one.

The stress vs. food question is tricky because they interact — stress can lower your threshold so foods that wouldn't normally trigger one suddenly do. What does your pattern look like? Are they happening at a particular time of day, or on certain days, or does it feel more random?

it's been kind of random which is what's frustrating. though I've also been spending a lot more time at screens lately which I know is a trigger for me. new job means a lot more staring at monitors lol. but I've also been wondering if my diet is a factor

Screen time is a very real migraine driver — the combination of blue light, the sustained focus, and often forgetting to blink or take breaks adds up. If you've significantly increased your screen hours with the new job, that alone could explain the uptick even if nothing else changed.

For diet and migraines, the classic culprits are: aged cheeses, red wine and alcohol generally, processed meats with nitrates, MSG, artificial sweeteners, and caffeine changes (either too much or withdrawal). Do any of those feature heavily in your current eating?

Also — and this is a real one — skipping meals and dehydration are huge migraine triggers and easy to do when you're busy and absorbed in work. Is your eating pretty regular throughout the day?

the skipping meals thing is probably me tbh. I get into a flow state and then suddenly it's 3pm and I haven't eaten. my doctor has been on me about this actually. oh and I actually started eating fish again a few weeks ago — my doctor said I needed more omega-3s for the migraines. still no meat though. she said the anti-inflammatory stuff might help

Your doctor is right on the omega-3 front — there's decent evidence that omega-3 fatty acids have anti-inflammatory effects that can reduce migraine frequency and severity over time. It's not an immediate fix, more like a long-term protective thing, but the research is fairly consistent. Fish is genuinely the most bioavailable source of EPA and DHA, more so than flaxseed or walnuts, which is probably why she recommended going that route rather than just taking supplements.

How are you feeling about eating fish again after not eating it for a while? Sometimes it takes a bit to adjust — both practically (learning to cook it) and just mentally readjusting your relationship to food.

And yes — the 3pm 'wait when did I last eat' situation is such a migraine recipe. Your brain runs on glucose, and if you're doing intensive focus work, it burns through it faster. A small alarm or habit stack to prompt lunch is genuinely medical-grade advice for migraine management, not just a nice idea.

honestly it's been fine? like I was vegetarian for a while so it felt like a bigger deal in my head but salmon is just... good lmao. I feel a little weird about it but also my migraines are bad enough that I'm willing to try things

That makes a lot of sense — when something is actively impacting your quality of life, the calculus changes. And 'it's been a while since I ate this, is this still me' is a real psychological thing with food identity, but it sounds like you're navigating it pretty practically.

Salmon is actually one of the better fish to start with — it's fatty so it has high omega-3 content, it's not particularly 'fishy' tasting, and it's easy to cook well. If you want to maximize the migraine benefit, fatty fish are what you're looking for: salmon, mackerel, sardines, anchovies. The leaner white fish (tilapia, cod) are fine but don't have nearly as much EPA/DHA.

For cooking salmon in particular: if you haven't already, the pan-sear method is very reliable even if you don't cook much. Hot pan, oil, skin side down for 3-4 minutes, flip for 1-2 minutes, done. Hard to mess up.

Are you taking any supplements on top of the dietary change, or relying on food sources alone?

just food for now, my doctor wanted to see if diet changes help first before adding more supplements. I already take magnesium which she said can also help with migraines? I read about that and apparently it's like really well supported

Yeah, magnesium for migraines is genuinely one of the better-supported supplements — there's actual randomized controlled trial evidence for it, which is more than you can say for most supplements. The theory is that migraineurs often have lower magnesium levels and it plays a role in nerve signaling and blood vessel regulation. The American Headache Society actually includes it in their guidelines now.

Glycinrate or malate forms are generally better tolerated than magnesium oxide (which is cheap but can cause GI issues). If you're taking oxide and noticing any stomach stuff, switching forms might help.

For the full migraine-diet protocol, the things that tend to help most in combination: omega-3s (you've got that covered with the fish), magnesium (covered), staying well hydrated, regular meal timing, and reducing the known triggers like aged cheese and alcohol if those are in your life.

How much water are you actually drinking during the workday? Because screen-intensive deep work is really dehydrating and it's easy to go hours without noticing.
I have a water bottle on my desk but I definitely don't drink enough. I feel like I should do one of those marked water bottles where it tells you how much you should have drunk by 10am, 12pm, etc

Those actually work really well for exactly this use case — the visual cue removes the need to remember, which is the hard part. A few brands make them with time markers, or you can just put rubber bands or little stickers at the levels yourself if you want a cheaper version.

For migraine purposes specifically: aim for around 2.5-3 liters a day, more if you're having coffee (caffeine is a mild diuretic). If you get to mid-afternoon and your urine is dark yellow, that's already a dehydration sign and you're playing catch-up. Pale yellow is the goal.

Honestly the combination you're building — omega-3s from fish, magnesium, better hydration, eating lunch at actual lunchtime — is a pretty solid protocol. These things work slowly and you might not notice a dramatic change immediately, but they tend to show up over 4-6 weeks as a general reduction in frequency rather than any single session being migraine-free. Keep track of your headaches if you aren't already, even just noting them in your phone calendar, so you can actually tell if there's a trend.

the tracking thing is a good idea. I think I just suffer through them and don't record anything and then I can't tell my doctor anything useful lol. I should probably download a headache tracker app or something

Migraine tracking apps are actually quite good — Migraine Buddy is probably the most full-featured one and is specifically designed for this. You log when a headache starts, severity, potential triggers (it prompts you with options), what you did for it, and how long it lasted. After a month or two it starts showing you patterns — your doctor will love you for having this data instead of 'um I think I had a few this month?'

Even just a basic notes app works if you find apps with too many fields annoying to fill in when you're mid-migraine. The minimum useful data is: date, severity 1-10, duration, anything notable that day (didn't eat, big screen day, stressed, etc.).

Hope the combination of fish + magnesium + hydration starts showing some results over the next month. It sounds like you're taking it seriously, which is the first step — migraines are one of those conditions that respond really well to consistent management even if there's no magic cure.
downloading Migraine Buddy right now. I feel like I actually have a plan instead of just white-knuckling through it. thanks for this
That's the best headspace to be in — migraines are so much less demoralizing when you feel like you have levers to pull. Hope the next few weeks show some improvement. Good luck!
